examples.text: إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ صَوْمًا فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنسِيًّا
examples.english: Lo! I have vowed a fast unto the Beneficent, and may not speak today to any mortal.

senses.gloss: to devote one's son to the service of God
to place in front of a combat
to see the danger, to see through the intentions of an enemy and be on one's guard
to make a vow to, profess before, profess a belief
a vow; verbal noun of نَذَرَ (naḏara, “to vow, to warn”) (form I)
warning, admonition, threat, irregular verbal noun of أَنْذَرَ (ʔanḏara) (form IV)

examples.text: وَلَقَدْ جَاءَ آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ النُّذُرُ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا
examples.english: And warning came to the house of the Pharaoh that they reproved.

etymology.text:
Akin to Aramaic נִדְרָא (niḏrā), Classical Syriac ܢܕܪܐ (neḏrā), Hebrew נֶדֶר (néḏer), Punic 𐤍𐤃𐤓 (ndr) and Ugaritic 𐎐𐎄𐎗 (ndr). It is argued that the sense vow is influenced from Jewish and Christian usage in Aramaic and Hebrew, the sense of “warning” as in أَنْذَرَ (ʔanḏara) and its verbal noun نُذُر (nuḏur) being more particular to Arabic, so the original meaning of the base stem needs to have been “to take heed”.

See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.
